symbols: cruciform figure with outstretched arms — the cross of Christ, the axis mundi connecting heaven and earth
radiating wings of red and gold — the seraphim of Isaiah's vision, the glory of God surrounding the locus of sacrifice
white cloth trailing downward — the burial shroud left behind at the resurrection, evidence of the empty tomb
stone-like setting — the tomb carved from rock, the underground space from which Christ rises
empty bench or slab — the empty place where the body lay, the definitive sign of resurrection (Mark 16:6)
description: A striking installation or mixed-media artwork set in a dark, cave-like underground space. At the center, a tall figure stands with arms outstretched in a cruciform pose, silhouetted in black against a brilliant burst of red, orange, and gold that radiates outward like wings or flames. The figure appears to be ascending, with a long white cloth or garment trailing from the lower body down to the stone floor. To the left, a wooden bench or platform supports what appears to be a magenta-colored cloth. The floor is rough stone or concrete, with rocks scattered about.
theological_description: This installation powerfully fuses the imagery of crucifixion and resurrection. The cruciform figure with outstretched arms evokes Christ on the cross, while the burst of radiant color surrounding it — red, gold, and flame-like — transforms the cross into a site of glory rather than defeat. The underground setting recalls the rock-hewn tomb of Joseph of Arimathea (Matthew 27:60), and the white cloth trailing to the ground echoes the linen wrappings found lying in the empty tomb by Peter and the Beloved Disciple (John 20:5-7). The winged, seraphic quality of the radiance alludes to Isaiah's vision of the Lord in the temple, where seraphim with six wings cried 'Holy, holy, holy' (Isaiah 6:2-3), suggesting that the cross is itself the throne of God's glory. The entire scene embodies the paradox at the heart of Christian theology: the cross, an instrument of shame and death, becomes through the resurrection the sign of cosmic victory. As Paul writes: 'Death has been swallowed up in victory. Where, O death, is your sting?' (1 Corinthians 15:54-55). The ascending motion of the figure and the trailing burial cloth declare that the tomb cannot hold the Lord of life.

scripture_refs.reason: The linen wrappings lying in the empty tomb, evoked by the white cloth trailing from the ascending figure.
He has risen; he is not here. See the place where they laid him — the empty stone setting of the installation.
The seraphim surrounding the throne of God, suggested by the winged, radiating form of fire and light.
Death swallowed up in victory — the triumphant emergence of the figure from the underground tomb-space.
Having disarmed the powers, Christ made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross.
